steps.text: Do not consider interactions with Tribal government officials only from your own cultural perspective.
Be sensitive to the cultural perspectives of all persons with whom you interrelate.
Recognize important Tribal priorities.
Plan to hold meetings in locations that do not impose significant financial burden on Tribal budgets, when possible.
Seek out group views and opinions.
Establish early communications and begin consultation as soon as practicable.
Consult in good faith throughout the agency's decision making process.
Take steps to ensure that properties of religious importance (e.g., traditional cultural properties) are considered during the NHPA Section 106 review process.
Coordinate consideration of properties of religious importance with AIRFA responsibilities.
Coordinate consultation with NHPA Section 106 consultation.

Provide 'affected Indian Tribe' timely and complete information regarding determinations or plans made with respect to site characterization, siting, development, design, licensing, construction, operation, regulation, or decommissioning of the repository.

Seek the views of the appropriate SHPO when identifying historic properties, evaluating the eligibility of historic properties for the National Register of Historic Places, and assessing effects of an undertaking on historic properties.
Consult with SHPOs when developing MOAs and PAs under NHPA Section 106.
Assume functions of an SHPO on Tribal lands.
Identify the Tribal contacts whom the staff has established relationships and other potential contacts and their roles and positions within the Tribal communities.
Discuss with the Tribal liaison whether the Tribal government considers the meeting privileged or confidential and thus nonpublic.
Identify the issues and topics for discussion and review, evaluate the agency information to be presented, and determine whether the Tribal meeting is a high level or staff level meeting.
Identify the desired outcomes for the meeting.
Clarify with the Tribal government whether the planned government to government meeting is public or nonpublic.
Document the meeting as publicly available.
Identify meeting participants who could include NRC staff, Tribal technical representatives, the NRC senior staff, or the Tribal head or Tribal Council.
Identify the appropriate rank of the NRC representative given the activity involved, the level of interaction, and the establish
Consider using various meeting formats, including presentations followed by discussions, listening sessions, small 'breakout' groups, and question and answer sessions.
Provide an opportunity for the Tribe to present a proposed meeting agenda in advance when planning meetings with Tribal participants.
Share the NRC’s proposed agenda with the Tribe.
Include suggestions from both the NRC and Tribal agendas in the final agenda.
Contact the Office of the General Counsel if a Tribal representative makes a statement pertaining to or asserting a claim.
Consider the prior experience, involvement, education, and training of the Tribe as related to the technical or licensing matter at hand.
Determine the need to provide explanations for technical and licensing terms.
Respect Tribal interests, cultural diversity, customs, and agendas.
Respect Tribal Council leaders as officials of another government.
Listen carefully and ask questions for clarification.
Consider what you say; do not promise what you may be unable to deliver.
Communicate with the Tribe early and often.
Familiarize yourself with the Tribe’s specific information and understand the history and current issues, concerns, and expertise of the Tribe or Tribes with whom you may work.
Hold face-to-face meetings to get to know the Tribe and its technical resource staff and council representatives.
Access the NMSS internal Web homepage.
Right click under the 'Agreement States' option on the left side of the homepage to open the drop down menu link.
Click on 'State & Tribal Phonebook'.
Select custom report parameters on the 'Custom Reports' screen.

preconditions.text: When planning where to hold meetings with Tribal representatives
The agency's regulatory actions or proposed projects have Tribal implications.

The Secretary of the Interior determines that the Tribal preservation program and the Tribe’s plan describes how the Tribal historic preservation official will carry out the functions that he or she proposes to assume.
Without the Tribal government’s written designation that an upcoming government-to-government meeting is considered privileged or confidential
Tribal representatives may not be familiar with the NRC’s terminology, technical vocabulary, acronyms, vernacular, and standard operating procedures.
User must be NRC staff with access to the NMSS internal Web homepage.

constraints.text: Tribal representatives may seem to place less priority than Federal employees do on timelines, schedules, and agendas.
Tribes often seek consensus in their decisionmaking and, therefore, place importance on face to face interactions.
Native Americans treat Tribal elders with great respect regardless of whether they hold elected office or an official position.
Practice open communication, adaptability, and open mindedness in interactions to develop positive working relationships with Tribal members.

Remember that NRC meetings are often attended by Tribal representatives and other parties with a vested interest in meeting topic(s), including State or local government representatives, representatives of local public interest groups, contractors or consultants of Federal agencies, other Federal agency employees, license applicants and their consultants, and other consulting Tribes.
The NRC staff should dress respectfully as for any business meeting with elected officials or distinguished representatives of a government.
The NRC staff should be respectful and not underestimate the knowledge of Tribal officials.
